model.description: Moonshot AI • Reasoning model from the Kimi family built for complex thinking tasks.

pick.rationale: Purdue enters as a dominant 2-seed with an elite 8th-ranked national profile featuring the nation's second-ranked offense, creating a catastrophic mismatch against Queens' porous 316th-ranked defense. The Boilermakers' +30.5 efficiency margin towers over Queens' negative -1.45 mark, representing a gap of nearly 200 ranking spots that aligns with historical 2/15 outcomes where favorites prevail approximately 94% of the time. While Queens captured a heartwarming first ASUN championship in program history, their defensive vulnerabilities and negative efficiency margin offer no credible path to overcome Purdue's Big Ten-tested pedigree and tournament momentum after defeating UCLA for the conference title.

reasoningStep.summary: Purdue's #2 offense vs Queens' #316 defense creates insurmountable mismatch

Queens ranks 181-199 with 78th offense (115.8) and 316th defense (117.3) for -1.45 margin

result.text: Key March Madness injuries to know while filling out your men’s NCAA Tournament bracket - The Athletic Premier League Champions League 2026 Men's World Cup F1 Tennis FA Cup Cricket La Liga Europa League Women's Football Olympics Global Sports Cycling Sailing Sports Business Boxing Golf Serie A Bundesliga International Football NFL NBA MLB World Baseball Classic NHL MLS Transfer News FPL League Cup Championship League One League Two Women's Euros NWSL College Sports NCAAF NCAAM Peak MMA Motorsports NASCAR Culture Memorabilia & Collectibles Gaming Sports Betting The PL Is Being Left Behind Welcome to Chelsea, Liam Rosenior PL Player of the Year Nominees Log In Subscribe Now Tickets by Viagogo Connections: Sports Edition REGION U.S. International Search Top News Podcasts NCAAM Bracket Scores & Schedule Teams Alabama Arizona Arkansas Duke Florida Gonzaga Houston Illinois Iowa State Kansas Louisville Miami (FL) Michigan State Michigan Nebraska North Carolina Purdue Saint Mary's St. John's Tennessee Texas Tech UConn Vanderbilt Virginia Wisconsin Standings Beat Our Expert's Bracket Tournament Projections Printable Bracket Predictions Game Men's March Madness LIVE First Four Updates Tournament Forecast Challenge Our Experts The Perfect Bracket Bracket Busters NBA Prospects to Watch Key Injuries To Know Key March Madness injuries to know while filling out your men’s NCAA Tournament bracket Patrick Ngongba II and Duke are the overall No. 1 seed, but a couple of injuries could make it more vulnerable. Grant Halverson / Getty Images By Jim Root March 17, 2026 Updated 3:19 pm EDT Share full article 2 The Athletic has live coverage of the First Four from 2026 Men’s March Madness. Roster attrition is a factor during the postseason in any sport. Optimistically, college basketball’s shorter regular season should mean the title picture is less altered by injuries than, say, the NBA. That is not necessarily the case this season, though. The injury bug has recently bitten two No. 1 seeds (Duke and Michigan), and a third (Arizona) played through a flurry of nagging injuries late in the season. Multiple All-Americans on dark-horse title contenders are out for the season. Advertisement Below is a repository of all the biggest injuries impacting the men’s NCAA Tournament. We have split them into two categories: Injuries worth monitoring (the players could or will return at some point during the tournament) and out for the season. Injuries to monitor Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba II, Duke Both Duke starters missed the ACC tournament. The Blue Devils managed to win that event regardless, but they might need some reinforcements to handle the competition they will see if they reach the Elite Eight and beyond. Ngongba, out with a foot injury, is arguably the more valuable piece due to his defensive presence, elite finishing and sneaky-strong passing. Fortunately for the Blue Devils, he is also much closer to returning than Foster, though

I have the ratings for both teams. Let me analyze them:

**Purdue:**
- KenPom rank: 8th
- Torvik rank: 8th
- Record: 26-8
- Adjusted efficiency margin: +30.5 (elite)
- Offense: 2nd nationally (131.0 adjusted)
- Defense: 36th nationally (100.5 adjusted)

**Queens:**
- KenPom rank: 181st
- Torvik rank: 199th
- Record: 21-13
- Adjusted efficiency margin: -1.45 (negative)
- Offense: 78th nationally (115.8 adjusted)
- Defense: 316th nationally (117.3 adjusted)

This is a massive mismatch. Purdue is a top-10 team nationally with the #2 offense in the country. Queens is a 15-seed with a negative efficiency margin and a very poor defense (316th nationally).

The gap is approximately 173-191 ranking spots, which is typical for a 2/15 matchup. Purdue's offense is elite, while Queens' defense is porous. This is a recipe for a blowout.

Optimistically, college basketball’s shorter regular season should mean the title picture is less altered by injuries than, say, the NBA. That is not necessarily the case this season, though. The injury bug has recently bitten two No. 1 seeds (Duke and Michigan), and a third (Arizona) played through a flurry of nagging injuries late in the season. Multiple All-Americans on dark-horse title contenders are out for the season. Advertisement Below is a repository of all the biggest injuries impacting the men’s NCAA Tournament. We have split them into two categories: Injuries worth monitoring (the players could or will return at some point during the tournament) and out for the season. Injuries to monitor Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba II, Duke Both Duke starters missed the ACC tournament. The Blue Devils managed to win that event regardless, but they might need some reinforcements to handle the competition they will see if they reach the Elite Eight and beyond. Ngongba, out with a foot injury, is arguably the more valuable piece due to his defensive presence, elite finishing and sneaky-strong passing. Fortunately for the Blue Devils, he is also much closer to returning than Foster, though

I have enough information now. Let me summarize what I've gathered:

**Purdue (2-seed):**
- KenPom/Torvik rank: 8th nationally
- Record: 26-8
- Elite offense: 2nd nationally in adjusted offense (131.0)
- Solid defense: 36th nationally (100.5)
- Adjusted efficiency margin: +30.5 (elite)
- Big Ten tournament champions
- No major injury concerns mentioned

**Queens (15-seed):**
- KenPom rank: 181st, Torvik: 199th
- Record: 21-13
- Mediocre offense: 78th nationally (115.8)
- Poor defense: 316th nationally (117.3)
- Negative adjusted efficiency margin: -1.45
- ASUN tournament champions (first in program history)
- Beat Central Arkansas 98-93 in OT for the title

**Analysis:**
This is a classic 2/15 matchup with a massive statistical gap. The 2/15 upset has happened only a handful of times in NCAA history (most recently Saint Peter's in 2022, FGCU in 2013, etc.). The gap between these teams is approximately 173-191 ranking spots.

Purdue's offense (2nd nationally) vs Queens' defense (316th) is a colossal mismatch. Queens simply doesn't have the defensive capability to slow down Purdue's elite scoring attack.

Queens is making their first-ever NCAA tournament appearance after winning their first ASUN title. While that's a great story, they don't have the statistical profile to suggest a credible upset threat.

The data strongly supports Purdue with very high confidence. 2 seeds win approximately 94% of the time against 15 seeds historically. With Purdue's elite metrics and Queens' porous defense, this is not a matchup where an upset is likely.

I should also note that Purdue has been relatively healthy and just won the Big Ten tournament, defeating UCLA 73-66 in the final. They're playing well at the right time.

I'll set confidence around 94-95% given the historical 2/15 win rate and the massive statistical disparity.

Based on my investigation, I have gathered comprehensive data on both teams. Let me summarize my analysis:

**Key Findings:**

**Purdue (2-seed):**
- Elite national ranking: 8th in both KenPom and Torvik
- Record: 26-8, Big Ten Tournament champions
- Offense: 2nd nationally in adjusted efficiency (131.0) - an elite scoring machine
- Defense: 36th nationally (100.5) - solid and tournament-tested
- Adjusted efficiency margin: +30.5 (elite tier)
- Just defeated UCLA 73-66 to win the Big Ten Tournament on March 14, 2026

**Queens (15-seed):**
- Low major ranking: 181st KenPom, 199th Torvik
- Record: 21-13, first-ever NCAA Tournament appearance
- Offense: 78th nationally (115.8) - mediocre against weak competition
- Defense: 316th nationally (117.3) - porous and vulnerable
- Adjusted efficiency margin: -1.45 (negative - below average)
- Won first ASUN title in program history, defeating Central Arkansas 98-93 in overtime

**Assessment:**
This matchup presents a gap of approximately 173-191 ranking spots between the teams. Purdue's #2-ranked offense faces Queens' 316th-ranked defense - a catastrophic mismatch for the 15-seed. Historically, 2 seeds defeat 15 seeds approximately 94% of the time (about 8 upsets in 140+ occurrences). Queens' negative efficiency margin and sub-300 defense provide no credible foundation for a historic upset against a Big Ten champion firing on all cylinders. Purdue enters healthy and in peak form after their conference tournament victory.
